files.name: bfs.algo
files.content:
bfsInit()

while not queue.isEmpty() do
  u ← queue.dequeue()
  visit(u)
  for each v in neighbors(u) do
    discover(u, v)
  end
end

reconstructPath(goal())

notes.text: **Setup in one step.** `bfsInit` builds the **queue**, the **dist**/**prev** maps and the **visited** set, then seeds them from the Start vertex. Keeping the bookkeeping in a helper lets this file read like the textbook algorithm — nothing here but the idea of BFS.
BFS explores in **waves**. The **queue** is the *frontier*: vertices we've found but not yet expanded. Watch its card pulse in the panel — it drives the whole search. We loop until it drains.

Take the vertex at the **front** of the queue. First-in-first-out order is the whole trick: nearer vertices are always expanded before farther ones, so the first time we reach a vertex is along a shortest path.
**Settle** `u` (turns green). Because of FIFO order its distance is already final, so `visit` can label it for good.
Fan out to every neighbour of `u` — the candidates for the next wave.
`discover` adds an unseen neighbour to the frontier (recording its distance and parent) and skips ones already seen. Step through it to watch the panel fill.
Walk the **prev** map backward from the Goal to light up the shortest path. `reconstructPath` is a **shared** helper — any search that records predecessors (BFS, Dijkstra, A*) can reuse it.

files.name: bfs-helpers.algo
files.content:
export function bfsInit() do
  queue ← panel.createQueue("queue")      // frontier: first in, first out
  visited ← panel.createSet("visited")    // never expand a vertex twice
  dist ← panel.createMap("dist")          // hops from the Start
  prev ← panel.createMap("prev")          // how we reached each vertex
  note(queue, "frontier")
  note(visited, "seen")
  note(dist, "distance")
  note(prev, "parent")
  spotlight(queue)                        // keep the frontier pulsing
  s ← source()
  dist[s] ← 0                             // the Start is zero hops away
  visited.add(s)
  queue.enqueue(s)
  mark(s, "info")
  setLabel(s, 0)
  scrollTo(s)
  showMessage("Start BFS from " + s, "info")
end

export function visit(u) do
  mark(u, "success")                      // settled: its distance is final
  setLabel(u, dist[u])
  scrollTo(u)
  showMessage("Settle " + u + " — distance " + dist[u] + " is now final", "success")
end

export function discover(u, v) do
  if v in visited then                    // the guard that keeps BFS linear
    showMessage(v + " already seen — skip", "warn")
    return
  end
  dist[v] ← dist[u] + 1                    // one more hop than u
  prev[v] ← u                              // remember the parent
  visited.add(v)
  queue.enqueue(v)                         // push to the back of the frontier
  mark(v, "info")
  mark(u, v, "info")                       // a tree edge (blue)
  setLabel(v, dist[v])
  scrollTo(u, v)
  showMessage("Discover " + v + " from " + u + " — distance " + dist[v], "info")
end

notes.text: Algorithm-specific setup. The four structures are created off-canvas with `panel.*`, so they stay out of the drawing but appear in the **Run data panel**, where you can watch them fill as the search runs.
`note(...)` pins a tiny label on each structure so a newcomer can read the panel at a glance: **frontier** (queue), **seen** (visited), **distance** (dist), **parent** (prev).
`spotlight(...)` makes the **queue** pulse for the whole search — a constant reminder that the frontier is the engine of BFS. When the search ends, `reconstructPath` moves the pulse to **prev**.
`visit` is pure visualization — colour `u` green, write its final distance, scroll it into view. The result is identical without it, but the run is far easier to follow.
The guard that keeps BFS **linear**: a vertex already in `visited` has its shortest distance, so we just flash a note and skip it — no mark is left behind, and it is never enqueued twice.
A BFS distance is just a **hop count**: one more than the vertex we arrived from. The new entries blink in the panel as they are written.

files.name: helpers.algo
files.content:
export function reconstructPath(t) do
  unspotlight(queue)                       // search over — drop the frontier focus
  spotlight(prev)                          // now we read the parent map
  showMessage("BFS done — trace back from " + t + " through prev", "success")
  for each n in nodes() do unmark(n) end   // clear the search tree...
  for each e in edges() do unmark(e.startVertex, e.endVertex) end
  cur ← t
  mark(cur, "warn")                        // ...and light up only the path
  scrollTo(cur)
  while cur in prev do
    p ← prev[cur]                          // one hop back toward the Start
    mark(p, cur, "warn")
    mark(p, "warn")
    scrollTo(p, cur)
    cur ← p
  end
  unspotlight(prev)
  showMessage("Shortest path from " + source() + " to " + t + " lit up", "success")
end

notes.text: A **general** helper, not tied to BFS. It reads the global **prev** map — the predecessor each vertex was reached from — so any search that records predecessors can call it to draw a path. Shared utilities like `isDAG` would live here too.
The search is done, so the spotlight moves off the (now-empty) **queue** and onto **prev** — the map we are about to read backward.

**Clean up before the reveal.** Every vertex and edge from the search tree is unmarked (distance labels stay), so the next few lines can light up *only* the shortest path with nothing competing for attention.
Follow parents from the target back toward the Start, recolouring each step **warn**. The Start has no parent, so `cur in prev` turns false there and the loop stops.
